skill.name: datetime
skill.description: Provides practical date and time command examples for generating current dates, timestamps, ISO 8601 values, UTC values, and file-safe names.
skill.summary: Generate current date, time, timestamp, UTC, and file-safe datetime values.
skill.author: DeevsDeevs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command usage and weak cryptographic algorithm patterns. Review found the external commands are expected local date, echo, and cp examples, while the weak-crypto findings on SKILL.md lines 3 and 53 are false positives from date-related text, with no evidence of cryptography or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Benign Local Shell Command Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes Bash examples that run date, echo, and cp. These are expected for a datetime command reference and do not show network access, credential access, persistence, or destructive behavior, but users should treat copied shell commands as executable instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in fenced Bash examples and match the skill purpose. No user-controlled interpolation, network calls, secret reads, or obfuscation were found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Is False Positive
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns at the description and format-code section. The reviewed lines describe datetime usage and date format codes, not hashing, encryption, or security logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural-language datetime documentation headings. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or any cryptographic operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples use command substitution to insert current datetime values into log text and filenames. This is normal shell usage for the skill, but it is still executable shell syntax.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is clearly present and executable, but it is limited to local date expansion inside benign examples.

content.user_title: Generate Reliable Date and Time Values
content.value_statement: Many tasks need consistent current dates, timestamps, and file-safe datetime strings. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code ready-to-use patterns for local date command output.
actual_capabilities: Shows date-only formats such as YYYY-MM-DD, YYYY/MM/DD, and DD-MM-YYYY.
Shows date and time formats for local time and ISO 8601 style output.
Provides UTC timestamp examples for cross-timezone workflows.
Lists common strftime format codes with examples.
Provides patterns for log timestamps and file-safe backup names.
Shows Unix epoch timestamps in seconds and milliseconds.
limitations: Uses the local system date command, so behavior can vary by operating system.
Does not convert arbitrary historical dates or parse natural language dates.
Does not provide timezone database lookup or calendar scheduling logic.
Examples are shell commands and should be reviewed before use in scripts.

use_cases.title: Add consistent log timestamps
use_cases.description: Generate a current timestamp format that can be prepended to logs or status messages.

use_cases.title: Create file-safe backup names
use_cases.description: Build filenames that include the current date and time without spaces or colons.

use_cases.title: Prepare API-ready UTC values
use_cases.description: Produce a current UTC ISO 8601 timestamp for requests, records, or documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Get today date
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple current date value.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the datetime skill to give me today date in YYYY-MM-DD format.

prompt_templates.title: Create a log timestamp
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate a current timestamp suitable for logs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the datetime skill to create a current local timestamp with date, hours, minutes, and seconds.

prompt_templates.title: Make a backup filename
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the datetime skill to suggest a file-safe timestamp for naming a backup created now.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a datetime format
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare formats for a workflow with local and UTC needs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the datetime skill to recommend formats for local logs, UTC API timestamps, and sortable backup filenames.

output_examples.input: Give me a current date format for a daily report.
output_examples.output: Use a YYYY-MM-DD date such as 2026-06-29. It sorts well and is easy to read.

output_examples.input: I need a timestamp for a backup filename.
output_examples.output: Use a compact value such as 20260629_170001. It avoids spaces and punctuation that can cause filename issues.

output_examples.input: I need a UTC timestamp for an API note.
output_examples.output: Use an ISO 8601 UTC value such as 2026-06-29T17:00:01Z. It clearly marks the timestamp as UTC.

best_practices: Use UTC timestamps when records are shared across systems or timezones.
Use file-safe formats without spaces or colons for filenames and backup names.
State whether a timestamp is local time or UTC when sharing it with others.
anti_patterns: Do not assume local time is correct for distributed systems or APIs.
Do not paste shell examples into production scripts without reviewing them.
Do not use human-readable month names when strict sorting is required.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps produce current date, time, timestamp, UTC, and filename-safe datetime formats.

faq.question: Does it run network requests?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed skill only documents local shell date command examples.

faq.question: Can it create Unix timestamps?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for Unix epoch seconds and millisecond-style timestamps.

faq.question: Can it format UTC timestamps?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a UTC ISO 8601 pattern using the local date command with UTC mode.

faq.question: Is it safe for filenames?
faq.answer: It includes formats without spaces or colons that are suitable for many filenames.

faq.question: What should users verify before using it?
faq.answer: Users should confirm their operating system supports the shown date command options.
